15 frame that is not a Basic Trigger frame and that causes a response after SIFS with an Address 1 field that does
16 not match the MAC address of the RD initiator. The RD responder shall not transmit any PPDUs with a
17 CH_BANDWIDTH that is wider than the CH_BANDWIDTH of the PPDU containing the frame(s) that deliv-
18
ered the RD grant.
19
20
21 An RD responder that transmits a Basic Trigger frame shall set the CS Required subfield to 1 and shall allocate
22 a number of streams for the RD initiator that is not smaller than the number of streams of the RD initiator's last
23 PPDU.
24
25
26 If an RD initiator sets the RDG/More PPDU field to 1 in a +HTC frame transmitted during a TXOP and sets
27 the AC Constraint subfield to 1 in that frame, the RD responder shall set the Preferred AC subfield of the Trig-
28 ger Dependent User Info field in the Trigger frame to the same AC as the last frame received from the RD ini-
29
tiator.
30
31

10.36 null data PPDU (NDP) sounding
33

35 10.36.6 Transmission of a VHT NDP
36
37
Insert the following after the 2nd paragraph:
38
39
40 If an HE STA transmits a VHT NDP where at least one of the intended recipients of the VHT NDP is an HE
41 STA, then the following conditions apply:
42
43 — If the bandwidth of an VHT NDP is less than or equal to 80MHz, the number of space-time streams
44 sounded as indicated by the TXVECTOR parameter NUM_STS shall not exceed the value indicated
45 in the Beamformee STS ≤80MHz subfield in the HE Capabilities element of any intended HE STA
46
recipient of the VHT NDP.
47
48 — If the bandwidth of an VHT NDP is greater than 80 MHz, the number of space-time streams sounded
49 as indicated by the TXVECTOR parameter NUM_STS shall not exceed the value indicated in the
50
Beamformee STS >80MHz subfield in the HE Capabilities element of any intended HE STA recip-
51
ient of the VHT NDP.

55 10.47 Target wake time (TWT)
56
57
10.47.1 TWT overview
58
59
60 Change the 1st paragraph as follows:
61
62
Target wake times (TWTs) allow STAs to manage activity in the BSS by scheduling STAs to operate at dif-
63
ferent times in order to minimize contention and to reduce the required amount of time that a STA utilizing
64
65 a power management mode needs to be awake. TWTs can be individual TWTs, which are described in 10.47
Copyright © 2020 IEEE. All rights reserved. 301
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.

## 第 302 页

IEEE P802.11ax/D8.0, October 2020
1 (Target wake time (TWT)) and 26.8.2 (Individual TWT agreements), or broadcast TWTs, which are
2 described in 26.8.3 (Broadcast TWT operation).
3
4
5 Change the 5th paragraph as follows:
6
7
STAs that request a TWT agreement are called TWT requesting STAs and the STAs that respond to their
8
requests are TWT responding STAs. A TWT requesting STA is assigned specific times to wake and
9
10 exchange frames with the TWT responding STA. A TWT requesting STA communicates wake scheduling
11 information to its TWT responding STA and the TWT responding STA devises a schedule and delivers
12 TWT values to the TWT requesting STA when a TWT agreement has been established between them. When
13
explicit TWT is employed, a TWT requesting STA wakes and performs a frame exchange and receives the
14
next TWT information in a response from the TWT responding STA as described in 10.47.3 (Explicit TWT
15
16 operation). When implicit TWT is used, the TWT requesting STA calculates the Next TWT by adding a
17 fixed value to the current TWT value as described in 10.47.4 (Implicit TWT operation). STAs need not be
18 made aware of the TWT values of other STAs. A TWT requesting STA and a TWT responding STA shall
19
set the Negotiation Type subfield to 0 in the TWT element of transmitted frames containing the TWT ele-
20
ment, except when the STAs are HE STAs. Additional TWT setup exchanges between HE STAs for individ-
21
22 ual TWT operation are defined in 26.8 (TWT operation).
23
24
Change paragraphs 7-9 as follows:
25
26
27 An S1G STA with dot11TWTOptionActivated equal to true and that operates in the role of TWT requesting
28 STA shall set the TWT Requester Support subfield to 1 in all S1G Capabilities elements that it transmits. An
29
S1G STA with dot11TWTOptionActivated equal to true and that operates in the role of TWT responding
30
STA shall set the TWT Responder Support subfield to 1 in all S1G Capabilities elements that it transmits.
31
32
33 A STA that is not an S1G STA and is not an HE STA and with dot11TWTOptionActivated equal to true and
34
that operates in the role of TWT requesting STA shall set the TWT Requester Support subfield to 1 in all
35
Extended Capabilities elements that it transmits. A STA that is not an S1G STA and is not an HE STA and
36
37 with dot11TWTOptionActivated equal to true and that operates in the role of TWT responding STA shall set
38 the TWT Responder Support subfield to 1 in all Extended Capabilities elements that it transmits.
39
40
If the TWT Responder Support subfield of the S1G Capabilities element, HE Capabilities element or
41
42 Extended Capabilities element received from its associated AP is equal to 1, a non-AP STA with
43 dot11TWTOptionActivated equal to true may transmit a TWT element to the AP with a value of Request
44 TWT, Suggest TWT or Demand TWT in the TWT Setup Command field and with the TWT Request field
45
equal to 1.
46
47
48 An AP with dot11TWTOptionActivated equal to true shall transmit a TWT element to a STA that is associ-
49 ated to the AP and from which it received a frame containing a TWT element that contained a value of
50
Request TWT, Suggest TWT or Demand TWT in the TWT Setup Command field and with the TWT
51
Request field equal to 1. The transmitted TWT element shall be included in the frame that is the appropriate
52
53 response frame to the received frame. The AP shall include a value of Accept TWT, Alternate TWT, Dictate
54 TWT or Reject TWT in the TWT Setup Command field of the response and shall set the TWT Request field
55 to 0. If the AP response’s TWT Setup Command field includes anything other than Accept TWT or Reject
56
TWT, the STA should send a new request for a TWT value by sending another frame that contains a TWT
57
element, modifying the parameters of the request to indicate, for example, an acceptance of a proposed alter-
58
59 nate TWT or dictated TWT value. If the STA receives a TWT response to a TWT request with the TWT
60 Setup Command field value of Accept TWT, then the STA has successfully completed a TWT setup with
61 that STA for the TWT Flow Identifier indicated in the TWT response and the STA becomes a TWT request-
62
ing STA and the STA may enter the doze state until the TSF matches the next TWT value of the STA, pro-
63
vided that the STA has indicated that it is in a power save mode and no other condition requires the STA to
64
65 remain awake. The AP becomes a TWT responding STA of the TWT requesting STA.
302 Copyright © 2020 IEEE. All rights reserved.
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.

## 第 303 页

IEEE P802.11ax/D8.0, October 2020
1 NOTE 1—A TWT responding STA might choose a TWT Flow Identifier for the TWT response that is different from the
2 TWT Flow Identifier of a received TWT request.
3
NOTE 2—A TWT requesting STA might renegotiate the TWT parameters of an existing TWT agreement by sending to
4
the TWT responding STA a TWT request with a Flow Identifier that corresponds to that TWT agreement. The TWT
5
response sent by the TWT responding STA containing the TWT Setup Command field of Accept TWT will indicate
6
whether the newly requested TWT parameters are accepted or whether the previously negotiated TWT parameters are
7
still in place.
8
9
10 Insert new paragraphs and table as follows after the 9th paragraph:
11
12 The result of an exchange of TWT Setup frames between a TWT requesting STA and a TWT responding
13
STA is defined in Table10-31a (TWT setup exchange command interpretation). The meaning of “Request
14
TWT” is that the requesting STA does not provide a set of TWT parameters for a TWT agreement, leaving
15
16 the choice of parameters to the responding STA, “Suggest TWT” indicates that the requesting STA offers a
17 set of preferred TWT parameters for a TWT agreement but might accept alternative TWT parameters that
18 the responding STA indicates and “Demand TWT” indicates that the requesting STA will currently accept
19
only the indicated TWT parameters for a TWT agreement. When transmitted by a responding STA, “Accept
20
TWT” indicates that the responding STA has initiated a TWT agreement with the given parameters, “Alter-
21
22 nate TWT” indicates a counter-offer of TWT parameters (although alternative TWT parameters might be
23 accepted as well) without the creation of a TWT agreement, “Dictate TWT” indicates that no TWT agree-
24 ment is created, but one is likely to be accepted only if the requesting STA transmits a new TWT setup
25
request with the indicated TWT parameters (i.e., no other TWT parameters will be accepted), and "Reject
26
TWT" transmitted by a responding STA as part of a negotiation for a new TWT agreement is used to indi-
27
28 cate that the negotiation has ended in failure to create a new TWT agreement.
29
30
31 Table 10-31a—TWT setup exchange command interpretation
32
33
34 TWT Setup Command
35 TWT Setup Command field field value in a response TWT condition after the completion of the
36 in an initiating frame frame exchange

38 Request TWT or Suggest No frame transmitted No new individual TWT agreement exists with the
39 TWT or Demand TWT TWT flow identifier corresponding to the TWT
40 flow identifier in the initiating frame. No new
41 individual TWT agreement exists.
42
43 Demand TWT Accept TWT An individual TWT agreement exists that uses the
44 TWT parameters identified in the initiating frame.
45 The TWT parameters in the response frame match
46 the TWT parameters of the initiating frame.
47
48 Suggest TWT or Request Accept TWT An individual TWT agreement exists and that uses
49 TWT the TWT parameters identified in the response
50 frame.
51
Demand TWT or Suggest Alternate TWT No individual TWT agreement exists with the
52
TWT associated TWT flow identifier. The responder is
53
offering an alternative set of parameters vs. those
54
indicated in the initiating frame. The requesting
55
STA can send a new request with any set of TWT
56

Change the last paragraph of 10.47.1 as follows:
26
27
28
A TWT requesting STA indicates which single channel it desires to use as a temporary primary channel
29
during a TWT SP by setting a single bit to 1 within the TWT Channel field of the TWT element, according
30
31 to the mapping described for that field. A TWT responding STA indicates which single channel the TWT
32 requesting STA is permitted to use as a temporary primary channel during a TWT SP by setting a single bit
33 to 1 within the TWT Channel field of the TWT element, according to the mapping described for that field. In
34
an S1G BSS, during During a TWT SP, access to a channel that is not the primary channel of the BSS shall
35
be performed according to the procedure described in 10.53 (Subchannel selective transmission (SST)).
36
37
38
In an HE BSS, during a trigger-enabled TWT SP, access to a channel that is not the primary channel of the
39
40 BSS shall be performed according to the procedure described in 26.8.7 (HE subchannel selective transmis-
41 sion).
42
43
44 If a STA has received a TWT Constraint Parameters element from another STA, it should not initiate a new
45 TWT agreement or join a broadcast TWT schedule with that STA if the total number of TWT sessions/
46 schedules between the two STAs would exceed the value of the Max TWT Sessions field in the TWT Con-
47
straint Parameters element.
48

52
should set the Target Wake Time field to an integer multiple of n+1 TUs (i.e., (Target Wake Time) mod
53
54 (n+1)=0) where n is the value from the Starting Target Wake Time Alignment field of the most recent

61 10.47.9 TWT parameter ranges
62
63
A STA that supports receiving two TWT elements in a TWT Setup frame sets the TWT Parameters Range
64
65 Support field to 1 in the Extended Capabilities elements that it transmits.
304 Copyright © 2020 IEEE. All rights reserved.
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.

## 第 305 页

IEEE P802.11ax/D8.0, October 2020
1 A TWT requesting STA may send a TWT request that contains two TWT elements to a TWT responding
2 STA if it has received an Extended Capabilities element from the TWT responding STA with the TWT
3
Parameters Range Support field equal to 1. The TWT requesting STA shall either set the TWT Setup Com-
4
mand fields of the two TWT elements to Suggest TWT or set the TWT Setup Command fields of the two
5
6 TWT elements to Demand TWT.
7
8
9 A TWT responding STA may send a TWT response that contains two TWT elements to a TWT requesting
10 STA if it has received an Extended Capabilities element from the TWT requesting STA with the TWT
11 Parameters Range Support field equal to 1. The TWT responding STA shall either set the TWT Setup Com-
12
mand fields of the two TWT elements to Alternate TWT or set the TWT Setup Command fields of the two
13
TWT elements to Dictate TWT.
14
15
16
17
18
19
If a STA sends a TWT request or a TWT response with two TWT elements:
20
21
— It shall set the TWT Flow Identifier field or the Broadcast TWT ID field of the two TWT elements to
22
23 the same value.
24
25 — For a TWT parameter of the proposed TWT agreement that is carried in the Target Wake Time field,
26 Nominal Minimum TWT Wake Duration field, and for the TWT wake interval parameter, the STA
27 may indicate either a single value or a range of values. For other TWT parameters, the STA shall
28
indicate a single value. To indicate a single value for a parameter, the STA sets the value of that
29
parameter to be the same in each TWT element. To indicate a range of values for a parameter, the
30
31 STA sets the value of that parameter differently in each TWT element. The range indicated for a
32 parameter is the range that starts with the lower value and includes all values up to the higher value,
33 irrespective of their order in the TWT elements, except for the TWT Wake Interval Mantissa and the
34
TWT Wake Interval Exponent fields. The range indicated for the TWT wake interval begins with the
35
lower value of the two TWT wake interval values and ends with the higher value of the two TWT
36
37 wake interval values, where the TWT wake interval is determined from the TWT Wake Interval
38 Mantissa and TWT Wake Interval Exponent fields as specified in 9.4.2.199 (TWT element).
39
40
41 If a TWT requesting STA sent a TWT request that contains two TWT elements to a TWT responding STA,
42 the TWT responding STA shall set each TWT parameter of the TWT response to a value selected from the
43
range of values provided in the TWT request, if the TWT Setup Command field of the TWT response is
44
Accept TWT and the parameter included a range in the corresponding TWT request.
45
46
47
If a TWT responding STA sent a TWT response that contains two TWT elements to a TWT requesting STA
48
49 with a TWT Setup Command field value of Dictate TWT, it implies that a TWT agreement is likely to be
50 accepted only if the requesting STA transmits a new TWT request with the TWT parameters set to a value
51 selected from the range of values provided in the TWT response. If a TWT responding STA sent a TWT
52
response that contains two TWT elements to a TWT requesting STA with a TWT Setup Command field
53
value of Alternate TWT, it implies a counter-offer of TWT parameters by the TWT responding STA, which
54
55 can be indicated in the form of a range of values (although alternative TWT parameters might be accepted as
56 well).

14 11.1.3.8.1 General

Change the 1st paragraph as follows:
17
18
19 A STA that supports the Multiple BSSID capability has dot11MultiBSSIDImplemented equal to true and
20 shall set to 1 the Multiple BSSID field of the Extended Capabilities elements that it transmits. Support for
21
the Multiple BSSID capability is mandatory for a FILS STA and non-AP HE STA. An AP that supports
22
enhancements related to the discovery and advertisement of a nontransmitted BSSID shall set the Enhanced
23
24 Multi-BSSID Advertisement Support bit in the Extended Capabilities element to 1 and is referred to as an
25 EMA AP. A 6 GHz AP with dot11MultiBSSIDImplemented equal to true and advertising a partial list of
26 nontransmitted BSSID profiles shall operate as an EMA AP. If an HE AP operating in the 2.4GHz or 5GHz
27
bands has dot11MultiBSSIDImplemented equal to true, advertises a partial list of nontransmitted BSSID
28
profiles and intends a non-AP STA to discover the complete list of nontransmitted BSSID profiles, where a
29
30 complete list of nontransmitted BSSID profile comprises only BSSIDs that are discoverable, then the HE
31 AP shall operate as an EMA AP.
32
33
A BSSID is discoverable if the AP includes information of that BSSID in its Beacon and Probe Response
34
frames (though not necessarily every frame).

47

31 11.1.3.8.3 Discovery of a nontransmitted BSSID profile
32
33
An AP or PCP may choose to include only a partial list of nontransmitted BSSID profiles in the Beacon
34
frame, S1G Beacon frame or DMG Beacon frame or to include different sets of nontransmitted BSSID
35
36 profiles in different Beacon frames, S1G Beacon frames or DMG Beacon frames. An AP corresponding to
37 the transmitted BSSID may choose to include only a partial list of nontransmitted BSSID profiles in an
38 unsolicited broadcast Probe Response frame or a Probe Response frame sent in response to a Probe Request
39
frame with Address 3 field set to wildcard BSSID and SSID set to wildcard. An AP advertising a complete
40
list of nontransmitted BSSID profiles shall set the Complete List Of NonTxBSSID Profiles field of
41
42 Extended Capabilities element to 1.
43
44
An EMA AP operating in the 2.4 GHz or 5 GHz band that transmits a Beacon or Probe Response frame
45
carrying a partial list of nontransmitted BSSID profiles should include in the frame a Reduced Neighbor
46
47 Report element carrying information for at least those nontransmitted BSSIDs that are not present in the
48 Multiple BSSID element carried in that frame.
49
50
A 6 GHz-only EMA AP that transmits a Beacon or broadcast Probe Response frame carrying a partial list of
51
52 nontransmitted BSSID profiles shall include a Reduced Neighbor Report element carrying information for at
53 least those nontransmitted BSSIDs that are discoverable and not present in the Multiple BSSID element car-
54 ried in that frame.
55
56
57 A 6 GHz-only EMA AP that transmits a FILS Discovery frame shall include a Reduced Neighbor Report
58 element carrying information on all nontransmitted BSSIDs in the multiple BSSID set that are discoverable.
59
60
The EMA AP does this to aid the fast discovery of all nontransmitted BSSIDs in the multiple BSSID set that
61
62 are discoverable.
63
64 NOTE—A FILS Discovery frame received from a 6 GHz AP with the Multiple BSSIDs Presence Indicator subfield
65 equal to 1 and not carrying a Reduced Neighbor Report element implies that the AP’s Beacon frame carries a complete

IEEE P802.11ax/D8.0, October 2020
1 list of nontransmitted BSSID profiles or that the information of the nontransmitted BSSID(s) is advertised in the 2.4
2 GHz or 5 GHz band by a co-located AP (see 11.53 (Out of band discovery of a 6 GHz BSS)).
3
4 An EMA AP advertising a partial list of BSSID profiles, shall include the Multiple BSSID Configuration
5
element (see 9.4.2.260 (Multiple BSSID Configuration element)) in its Beacon frame, S1G Beacon frame,
6
or DMG Beacon frame and in any Probe Response frame it sends to indicate the configuration of the
7
8 multiple BSSID set.
9
10 An AP shall set the BSSID Count field of the Multiple BSSID Configuration element to indicate the number
11
of active BSSIDs in the multiple BSSID set, and shall set the Full Set Rx Periodicity field to indicate the
12
number of beacons a scanning STA is required to receive in order to discover all the active nontransmitted
13
14 BSSIDs in the set. An AP corresponding to the transmitted BSSID shall respond with a Probe Response
15 frame carrying one or more Multiple BSSID elements that include, at a minimum, the profiles for the
16 nontransmitted BSSIDs requested by the soliciting Probe Request frame.
17
18 NOTE—A nontransmitted BSSID profile is requested if, in the soliciting Probe Request frame, the SSID or BSSID
19 matches the SSID or the BSSID respectively of the BSS corresponding to the nontransmitted BSSID.
20
21 An unassociated non-AP STA may transmit a Probe Request frame containing a Known BSSID element
22
(see 9.4.2.261 (Known BSSID element)) and addressed to an EMA AP corresponding to the transmitted
23
BSSID to gather information on nontransmitted BSSIDs that it has not discovered.
24
25
26 An EMA AP that transmits a Probe Response frame in response to a Probe Request frame containing a
27 Known BSSID element shall, at a minimum, include the nontransmitted BSSID profiles not known to the
28
requesting STA unless the AP is unable to fit all of them in the response frame.
29
30
31 An EMA AP that includes a partial list of nontransmitted BSSID profiles in its Beacon frame, S1G Beacon
32 frame, or DMG Beacon frame, shall advertise a particular nontransmitted BSSID profile in a repeating
33 pattern such that the profile is present in at least one beacon in a sequence of beacons indicated by the Full
34
Set Rx Periodicity field of the Multiple BSSID Configuration element unless the membership of the multiple
35
BSSID set changes. An EMA AP shall include a nontransmitted BSSID profile in the DTIM beacon of that
36
37 BSS so that STAs associated to that AP can receive the profile (and any updates to the BSS configuration)
38 without having to wake up for additional beacons. An EMA AP shall select the DTIM interval for a
39 nontransmitted BSSID as a multiple of the value carried in the Full Set Rx Periodicity field of the Multiple
40
BSSID Configuration element. AnnexAA provides several example configurations.
41
42
NOTE 1—An AP corresponding to a nontransmitted BSSID advertises any changes to its BSS operational parameters
43
during the beacon interval that follows the profile's DTIM beacon. For example, an AP corresponding to the nontrans-
44
mitted BSSID can send a broadcast Disassociation frame to disassociate all STAs associated with it.
45
46 NOTE 2—In order to aid fast discovery of nontransmitted BSSIDs via passive scanning, it is recommended that an AP
47 select a small value for the Full Set Rx Periodicity field.

in which the element is present, except for the Quiet element, which shall take effect only in the Beacon

frame or DMG Beacon frame that contains it and not carry forward as a part of the inheritance. An AP or
64
65 PCP is not required to include all supported nontransmitted BSSID profiles in a Probe Response frame, and

30 The Partial Virtual Bitmap field of the TIM element carried in the Beacon, S1G Beacon, DMG Beacon, or

34

36 S1G STA is 2n (see 9.4.2.5 (TIM element)). The decimal value of the 11 LSBs of the AID assigned to an
37 S1G STA shall be greater than 2n. The Encoded Blocks that contain these first 2n AIDs (if any) shall precede

45
46
47 Based upon its knowledge of the capability of associated non-AP STAs to support the multiple BSSID

49 the traffic indication virtual bitmap, an AP shall encode the Partial Virtual Bitmap and the Bitmap Control
50
field of the TIM element using one of the three methods (Method A, Method B or Method C) defined in
51
9.4.2.5.1 (General). Specifically, a non-S1G AP shall use Method B if it determines that the bit for each
52
53 associated non-AP STA in the traffic indication virtual bitmap that is reconstructed by each non-AP STA
54 from the received TIM element encoded using Method B is set correctly. Otherwise, a non-S1G AP shall use
55 Method A and an S1G AP shall use Method C.
56
57 NOTE—If all the recipients of the TIM element are STAs that support the multiple BSSID capability, for example when
58 the TIM element is carried in HE beacon, ER beacon, FILS Discovery frame or OPS frame where all the addressees are
59 non-AP HE STAs, the transmitting AP uses Method B to encode the Partial Virtual Bitmap and the Bitmap Control

40 11.2 Power management
41
42
43 11.2.3 Power management in a non-DMG infrastructure network
44
45 11.2.3.2 Non-AP STA power management modes
46
47
48 Change the 1st paragraph as follows:
49
50
A non-AP STA can be in one of two power management modes:
51
52 — Active mode: The STA receives and transmits frames at any time if the STA is in awake state. The A
53 non-HE STA remains in the awake state. An HE STA remains in the awake state unless the STA is
54
unavailable. A STA that is unavailable is not capable of receiving PPDUs. A STA is permitted to be
55
unavailable as described in 26.14.3 (Opportunistic power save), 26.14.1 (Intra-PPDU power save for
56
57 non-AP HE STAs) and 26.8.4.4 (TWT Information frame exchange for flexible wake time).
58
— Power save (PS) mode: The STA enters the awake state to receive or transmit frames. The STA
59
remains in the doze state otherwise.
60
61
62 11.2.3.6 AP operation
63
64
65 Change the 1st paragraph as follows:

1 An AP shall maintain for each currently associated STA a Power Management status that indicates in which
2 power management mode the STA is currently operating. APs that implement and signal their support of
3
APSD shall maintain for each currently associated STA an APSD and an access policy status that indicates
4
whether the STA is presently using APSD and shall maintain the schedule (if any) for the STA. An AP shall,
5
6 depending on the power management mode of the STA, temporarily buffer BUs destined to the STA. An AP
7 implementing APSD shall, if a STA is using APSD and is in PS mode, temporarily buffer BUs destined to
8 that STA. No BUs addressed directly to STAs operating in the active mode shall be buffered for power man-
9
agement reasons. An HE AP should not transmit to an HE STA if the STA might be unavailable, as defined
10
in 26.8.4.4 (TWT Information frame exchange for flexible wake time) and 26.14.3 (Opportunistic power
11
12 save), unless the transmission is solicited by the STA.

16
17 The following rules describe the operation:
18
f) When dot11FMSActivated is false, the AP shall transmit all buffered non-GCR-SP (11ak)non-
19
20 SYNRA group addressed BUs immediately after every DTIM or during broadcast TWT SPs within
21 that beacon interval as defined in 26.8.3.2 (Rules for TWT scheduling AP).

23
24 cast stream, the AP shall transmit all non-GCR-SP (11ak)non-SYNRA group addressed BUs

within that beacon interval as defined in 26.8.3.2 (Rules for TWT scheduling AP).

29 The More Data subfield of each group addressed frame shall be set to indicate the presence of fur-
30 ther buffered non-GCR-SP group addressed BUs that will be delivered using MPDUs with an RA
31
other than a SYNRA. If the AP is unable, before the primary or secondary TBTT following the

33
34 ered using MPDUs with an RA other than a SYNRA, then the AP shall set the bit for AID 0 (zero) in
35 the TIM element to 1 for a single BSSID or set the corresponding group address bit to 1 for multiple
36 BSSIDs, as defined in 9.4.2.5 (TIM element), and when dot11FMSActivated is true, shall set the
37
appropriate bits in the FMS Descriptor element as described in 9.4.2.74 (FMS Descriptor element)
38
to indicate for which non-GCR-SP non-SYNRA group addresses there are still buffered BUs, until
39
40 all buffered non-GCR-SP group addressed BUs that will be delivered using MPDUs with an RA
41 other than a SYNRA have been transmitted.
42
43 When the AP transmits an STBC DTIM or TIM Beacon frame, the AP shall retransmit all non-
44 GCR-SP group addressed BUs that will be delivered using MPDUs with an RA other than a
45 SYNRA and that were transmitted following the non-STBC DTIM or TIM Beacon frame except
46
that they are transmitted using the basic STBC MCS. It may be the case that a complete set of buff-
47
ered non-GCR-SP non-SYNRA group addressed BUs is sent over a period of time during which
48
49 non-STBC and STBC transmissions are interleaved, but the transition from non-STBC group
50 addressed transmissions to STBC group addressed transmissions shall be preceded by the transmis-
51 sion of an STBC Beacon frame and the transition from STBC group addressed transmissions to non-
52
STBC group addressed transmissions shall be preceded by the transmission of a non-STBC Beacon
53
frame.
54
55 g) When the AP receives a PS-Poll frame from a STA that is in PS mode, it shall forward to the STA a
56
single buffered BU. The AP shall respond after a SIFS either with a Data or Management frame, or
57
with an Ack frame; in which case the corresponding Data or Management frame is delayed. Until
58
59 the transmission of this BU either has succeeded or is presumed failed (when maximum retries are
60 exceeded), the AP shall acknowledge but ignore all PS-Poll frames from the same STA. This pre-
61 vents a retried PS-Poll frame from being treated as a new request to deliver a buffered BU.
62
63
For a STA using U-APSD, the AP transmits one BU destined for the STA from any AC that is not
64
65 delivery-enabled in response to PS-Poll frame from the STA. The AP should transmit the BU from

1 the highest priority AC that is not delivery-enabled and that has a buffered BU. When all ACs asso-

stream.

51
More Data subfield of the appropriate type (non-STBC or STBC) of group addressed BUs indicates

53 there are no further buffered group addressed BUs of that type, or until a TIM is received indicating

QoS Info field QoS Capability element to 1, then if it receives an Ack frame from its AP with the

65 More Data bit equal to 1, the STA shall operate exactly as if it received a TIM with its AID bit equal

11.2.3.15 TIM Broadcast
21
22
23 Change the 3rd paragraph as follows (splitting it into two paragraphs):
24
25
A non-AP STA may activate the TIM broadcast service by including a TIM Broadcast Request element in a
26
TIM Broadcast Request frame, Association Request frame or Reassociation Request frame that is transmit-
27
28 ted to the AP, which specifies the requested interval between TIM frame transmissions (the TIM broadcast
29 interval). On receipt of a properly formatted TIM Broadcast Request element in a TIM Broadcast Request
30 frame, Association Request frame or Reassociation Request frame, the AP shall include a TIM Broadcast
31
Response element in the corresponding TIM Broadcast Response frame, Association Response frame or
32
Reassociation Response frame, when dot11TIMBroadcastActivated is true. A non-AP STA shall transmit a
33
34 TIM Broadcast Request only if the associated AP has indicated support for TIM Broadcast by setting the
35 TIM Broadcast field of the Extended Capabilities elements that it transmits to 1.
36
37 NOTE—An OPS AP that transmits TIM frames as described in 26.14.3 (Opportunistic power save) is expected to
38 encode the TIM bits such that an associated non-AP STA that does not support OPS operation can use the information
39 received in the TIM frame as it would do when receiving a TIM frame transmitted following the TIM Broadcast proce-
40 dure.
41
42 When the requested TIM broadcast interval is acceptable, the AP shall include a TIM Broadcast Response
43
element specifying the requested TIM broadcast interval and a Status field indicating “Accept” when no
44
valid TSF timestamp is present in the TIM frames, or “Accept, valid timestamp present in TIM frames”
45
46 when a valid TSF timestamp is present in the TIM frames. When the AP overrides the requested TIM broad-
47 cast interval, it shall include a TIM Broadcast Response element specifying a different TIM broadcast inter-
48 val and a Status field indicating “Overridden” when no valid TSF timestamp is present in the TIM frames, or
49
“Overridden, valid timestamp present in TIM frames” when a valid TSF timestamp is present in the TIM
50
frames, and include in the TIM Broadcast Response element the smallest TIM broadcast interval that is cur-
51
52 rently active. Otherwise, the AP shall include a TIM Broadcast Response element with a Status field indicat-
53 ing “Denied.” The Status field in a TIM Broadcast Response element that is included in an Association
54 Response frame or Reassociation Response frame has implications only for the TIM Broadcast negotiation.
55
56
57 Change the 11th paragraph as follows:
58

frame(s) when a critical update occurs to any of the elements inside the Beacon frame. The following events

32 11.2.6 SM power save
33
34
Insert the following after the 2nd paragraph:
35
36
37 The basic rules for a STA are defined below. Additional rules for an HE STA in dynamic SM power save
38
mode that sets the HE Dynamic SM Power Save subfield to 1 in the HE MAC Capabilities Information field
39
in the HE Capabilities element it transmits are defined in 26.14.4 (HE dynamic SM power save).
40
41
42 Change the 3rd and subsequent paragraphs as follows:
43
44
In dynamic SM power save mode, the STA enables its multiple receive chains when it receives the start of a
45
46 frame exchange sequence addressed to it. Such a frame exchange sequence shall start with a single-spatial
47 stream individually addressed frame that is not a Trigger frame and that requires an immediate response and
48 that is addressed to the STA in dynamic SM power save mode. An RTS/CTS sequence may be used for this
49
purpose. The STA shall, subject to its spatial stream capabilities (see 9.4.2.55.4 (Supported MCS Set field)
50
and 9.4.2.157.3 (Supported VHTMCS and NSS Set field)) and operating mode (see 11.41 (Notification of
51
52 operating mode changes)), be capable of receiving a PPDU that is sent using more than one spatial stream a
53 SIFS after the end of its response frame transmission. The STA switches to the multiple receive chain mode
54 when it receives the frame addressed to it and switches back may switch back to the single receive chain
55
mode immediately when after the end of the frame exchange sequence ends.
56
57
NOTE—A STA in dynamic SM power save mode cannot distinguish between an RTS/CTS sequence that precedes a
58
MIMO transmission and any other RTS/CTS and, therefore, always enables its multiple receive chains when it receives
59
if it responds to the RTS addressed to it.
60
61
The STA can determine the end of the frame exchange sequence through any of the following:
62
63 — It receives an individually addressed frame addressed to another STA.
64
65 — It receives a frame with a TA that differs from the TA of the frame that started the TXOP.
Copyright © 2020 IEEE. All rights reserved. 317
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.

## 第 318 页

IEEE P802.11ax/D8.0, October 2020
1 — It receives a PPDU and classifies the PPDU as inter-BSS PPDU (see 26.2.2 (Intra-BSS and inter-
2 BSS PPDU classification)).
3
4 — It receives an HE MU PPDU where the RXVECTOR parameter BSS_COLOR is the BSS color of
5 the BSS in which the STA is associated, the RXVECTOR parameter does not have any STA_ID of
6 an RU that identifies the STA as the recipient or one of the recipients of the RU (see 26.11.1
7
(STA_ID)), and the BSS Color Disabled subfield in the most recently received HE Operation ele-
8
ment from the AP with which the STA is associated is 0.
9
10
— The CS mechanism (see 10.3.2.1 (CS mechanism)) indicates that the medium is idle at the TxPIFS
11
slot boundary (defined in 10.3.7 (DCF timing relations)).
12
13
14 In static SM power save mode, the STA maintains only a single receive chain active.
15
16
17 The STA may use the SM Power Save frame to communicate its SM power save state. The STA may also
18 use SM Power Save subfield in the HT Capabilities element of its (Re)Association Request frame or the SM
19 Power Save subfield in the HE 6 GHz Band Capabilities element of its (Re)Association Request frame to
20
achieve the same purpose. The latter allows the STA to use only a single receive chain immediately after
21
(re)association.
22
23
24 A STA that has one or more TDLS links shall not operate in SM power save mode.
25
26
27 Changes to the number of active receive chains are made only after the SM power save mode indication has
28 been successfully delivered (i.e., by acknowledgment of a frame carrying the HT Capabilities element or by
29 acknowledgment of a frame carrying the HE 6 GHz Band Capabilities element or by acknowledgment of a
30
SM Power Save frame). The SM power save mode indication shall be transmitted using an individually
31
addressed frame.

11.7 TPC procedures
38
39
40
Change “Local Maximum Power For X MHz subfield” to “Maximum Power For X MHz subfield”
41
(where X is 20, 40 80 or 16/80+80) throughout 11.7.
42
43
44 11.7.1 General
45
46
47 Change the 1st paragraph as follows:
48
49
Regulations that apply to the 5 GHz band in most regulatory domains require RLANs operating in the 5 GHz
50
band or 6 GHz band to use transmitter power control, involving specification of a regulatory maximum
51
52 transmit power and a mitigation requirement for each allowed channel. This standard describes such a mech-
53 anism, referred to as transmit power control (TPC).
54
55
11.7.5 Specification of regulatory and local maximum transmit power levels
56
57
58 Change as follows:
59
60
A STA shall determine a regulatory maximum transmit power for the current channel by selecting the
61
62 minimum of the following:
63
— Any regulatory maximum transmit power received in a Country element from the AP in its BSS,
64
65 PCP in its PBSS, another STA in its IBSS, or a neighbor peer mesh STA in its MBSS
Copyright © 2020 IEEE. All rights reserved. 319
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.

## 第 320 页

IEEE P802.11ax/D8.0, October 2020
1 — If the STA is extended spectrum management capable, any regulatory client maximum transmit
2 power received in a Transmit Power Envelope element from the AP in its BSS, another STA in its
3
IBSS, or a neighbor peer mesh STA in its MBSS
4
5 — Any regulatory maximum transmit power for the channel in the current regulatory domain known by
6 the STA from other sources
7
8
9 A STA shall determine a local maximum transmit power for the current channel by selecting the minimum
10 of the following:
11
— Unless the STA is extended spectrum management capable and has received a Transmit Power
12
13 Envelope element for a channel width of 20 MHz and 40 MHz, any local maximum transmit power
14 received in the combination of a Country element and a Power Constraint element from the AP in its
15 BSS, PCP in its PBSS, another STA in its IBSS, or a neighbor peer mesh STA in its MBSS
16
17 — If the STA is extended spectrum management capable, any local maximum transmit power received
18 in a Transmit Power Envelope element from the AP in its BSS, another STA in its IBSS, or a
19 neighbor peer mesh STA in its MBSS
20
21 — Any local maximum transmit power for the channel in the current regulatory domain known by the
22 STA from other sources
23
24 NOTE 1—A STA might receive a maximum transmit power in a Transmit Power Envelope element from the AP in its
25 BSS, another STA in its IBSS, or a neighbor peer mesh STA in its MBSS in various management frames, including Bea-
26 con frames, Probe Response frames, FILS Discovery frames and (prior to a channel switch) New Transmit Power Enve-
27 lope elements (in Channel Switch Wrapper elements, Future Channel Guidance elements, Channel Switch
28 Announcement elements/frames or Extended Channel Switch Announcement elements/frames). Other sources from
29 which a STA might receive a maximum transmit power for a channel include Reduced Neighbor Report elements (20
30 MHz PSD subfield) sent by a (co-located) AP. If this information is received by a STA, any requirements on its usage
31 depend on local regulations known at the STA (see AnnexE.2).
32
NOTE 2—The determination of a maximum transmit power from Transmit Power Envelope element(s) is specified in
33
10.22.4 (Operation with the Transmit Power Envelope element).
34
35
36 The Local Power Constraint field of any transmitted Power Constraint element and each Local Maximum
37 Transmit Power For XMHz field (where X = 20, 40, 80, or 160/80+80) in the Transmit Power Envelope
38
element shall each be set to its respective value that allows the mitigation requirements to be satisfied in the
39
current channel.
40
41
NOTE—The local maximum transmit power for the channel needs to meet the mitigation requirements for the channel
42
in the current regulatory domain. The conservative approach is to set the local maximum transmit power level equal to
43
the regulatory maximum transmit power level minus the mitigation requirement. However, it might be possible to satisfy
44
the mitigation requirement using a higher local maximum transmit power level. A lower local maximum transmit power
45
level might be used for other purposes (e.g., range control, reduction of interference).
46
47
48 The regulatory and local maximum transmit powers may change in a STA during the life of an infrastructure
49 BSS and an MBSS. However, network stability needs to be considered when deciding how often or by how
50 much these maximums are changed. The regulatory and local maximum transmit powers shall not change
51
during the life of an IBSS.
52
53
54 An AP, IBSS STA, or mesh STA that is not operating in the 6 GHz band shall advertise the regulatory
55 maximum transmit power for that STA’s operating channel in Beacon frames and Probe Response frames
56 using a Country element. An AP, IBSS STA or mesh STA shall advertise the local maximum transmit power
57
for that STA’s operating channel in Beacon frames and Probe Response frames using the combination of a
58
Country element and a Power Constraint element.
59
60
61 If an AP, IBSS STA, or mesh STA that is not operating in the 6 GHz band is extended spectrum
62
management capable, it shall advertise the local maximum transmit power for that STA’s operating channel
63
in Beacon frames and Probe Response frames using one Transmit Power Envelope element for each distinct
64
65 value of the Local Maximum Transmit Power Unit Interpretation subfield that is supported by the BSS,
320 Copyright © 2020 IEEE. All rights reserved.
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.

## 第 321 页

IEEE P802.11ax/D8.0, October 2020
1 IBSS, or MBSS, respectively. Each Transmit Power Envelope element shall include a local power constraint
2 for all channel widths supported by the BSS.
3
4
5 If an AP, IBSS STA, or mesh STA is operating in the 6 GHz band, it shall include Transmit Power Envelope
6 element(s) in Beacon and Probe Response frames as follows:
7
— For each distinct combination of values of the Maximum Transmit Power Interpretation subfield and
8
9 Maximum Transmit Power Category subfield that are supported by the BSS, IBSS, or MBSS,
10 respectively, one Transmit Power Envelope element is included that indicates the regulatory client
11 maximum transmit power for the STA’s operating channel
12
13 — At a minimum, a regulatory client maximum transmit power is indicated where the Maximum Trans-
14 mit Power Category subfield is set to 0 (Default). If different regulatory limits apply to different cat-
15 egories, the value of the limit advertised for Default category shall be the minimum of those limits.
16
17 — If a local maximum transmit power is different from (lower than) a regulatory client maximum trans-
18 mit power for the same combination of values of the Maximum Transmit Power Interpretation sub-
19 field and Maximum Transmit Power Category subfield, a Transmit Power Envelope element is
20
included that indicates that local maximum transmit power.
21
22 — Each Transmit Power Envelope element that is included indicates a power constraint for all channel
23 widths supported by the BSS (if an EIRP constraint is advertised) or for all 20 MHz channels within
24
the bandwidth of the BSS (if an EIRP PSD constraint is advertised).
25
26
NOTE 1—The requirement in the second bullet ensures that a STA, even if it does not know how to interpret the Regu-
27
latory Info subfield (in the HE Operation element) and/or non-default values of the Maximum Transmit Power Category
28
subfield (in Transmit Power Envelope elements) for the current country, can determine a regulatory client maximum
29
transmit power for use with the AP. This default value might be lower than the actual regulatory client maximum trans-
30
mit power for specific categories.
31
32 NOTE 2—The regulatory client maximum transmit power is the regulatory limit for client devices such as non-AP
33 STAs, which might be different from the regulatory limit for APs.
34
35 NOTE 3—STAs operating in the 6 GHz band are extended spectrum management capable (see 10.22.3 (Operation with
36 operating classes)).
37
NOTE 4—If a Transmit Power Envelope element in a Beacon or Probe Response frame in the 6 GHz band indicates
38
EIRP PSD limits, the value of N (see Table9-276a (Meaning of Maximum Transmit Power Count subfield if the Maxi-
39
mum Transmit Power Interpretation subfield is 1 or 3)) is either equal to 0, or equal to the BSS bandwidth in MHz
40
divided by 20 (1, 2, 4 or 8 for 20, 40, 80 or 160/80+80 MHz BSS bandwidth, respectively).
41
42 NOTE 5—An AP, IBSS STA, or mesh STA does not need to send Transmit Power Envelope elements indicating maxi-
43 mum transmit EIRP if it sends Transmit Power Envelope elements indicating maximum transmit PSD and those PSD
44 values are sufficient to ensure regulatory limits on total EIRP are always met for all transmission bandwidths of STAs in
45 the BSS.
46
47
STAs that are extended spectrum management capable and have dot11RadioMeasurementActivated equal to
48
49 true should be able to reduce their EIRP to 0 dBm.
50
51 NOTE—When the local maximum transmit power is set by an AP for radio resource management, a typical low value
52 for the local power constraint is 0 dBm. A STA that cannot reduce its transmit power to this level or below will not be
53 able to associate to the AP.
54
55 The PCP in a PBSS shall advertise both the regulatory maximum transmit power and the local maximum
56 transmit power for its operating channel in Announce and Probe Response frames (using a Country element
57
for the regulatory maximum and a combination of a Country element and a Power Constraint element for the
58
local maximum). In addition, it should advertise both regulatory and local maximum transmit power in
59
60 DMG Beacon frames.
61
62
When dot11SpectrumManagementRequired is false and dot11RadioMeasurementActivated is true, an AP or
63
a PCP may include a Power Constraint element and a Transmit Power Envelope element in Beacon, DMG
64
65 Beacon, Announce, and Probe Response frames.

This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.

IEEE P802.11ax/D8.0, October 2020
1 11.7.6 Transmit power selection
2
3
Change as follows:
4
5
6 A STA may select any transmit power for transmissions in a channel within the following constraints:
7
— A STA shall determine a regulatory maximum transmit power and a local maximum transmit power
8
for a channel in the current regulatory domain before transmitting in the channel.
9
10 — An AP shall use a transmit power less than or equal to the regulatory maximum transmit power level
11
for the channel. The AP shall also meet any regulatory mitigation requirement.
12
13 — A non-AP STA shall use a transmit power less than or equal to the minimum or the local maximum
14 transmit power level and regulatory client maximum transmit power for the channel.

17 11.10 Radio measurement procedures
18
19
11.10.14 Multiple BSSID set
20
21
22 Change the 1st paragraph as follows:
23
24
A multiple BSSID set is characterized as follows:
25
26 — All members of the set use a common operating class, channel, Channel Access Functions, receive
27 antenna connector, and transmit antenna connector.
28
29 — The set has a maximum range of 2n for at least one n, where 1 ≤ n ≤ 46
30
• 1 ≤ n ≤ 8 if dot11MultiBSSIDImplemented is true
31
• 1 ≤ n ≤ 46 if dot11MultiBSSIDImplemented (if present) is false and dot11RMMeasurementPilo-
32
33 tActivated is nonzero
34
— Members of the set have the same 48-n bits (BSSID[0:(47-n)]) in their BSSIDs.
35
36 — All BSSIDs within the multiple BSSID set are assigned in a way that they are not available as MAC
37 addresses for STAs using a different operating class, channel, receive antenna connector, or transmit
38 antenna connector.
39
40 NOTE—For example, if the APs within BSSs with BSSIDs 16, 17, and 27 share the same operating class, channel,
41 receive antenna connector, and transmit antenna connector, and the range of MAC addresses from 16–31 inclusive are
42 not assigned to other STAs using a different receive antenna connector or transmit antenna connector, then the BSSIDs
43 16, 17, and 27 are members of a multiple BSSID set. The set is described by n = 4 (2n = 16) with BSSIDs in the range
44 16–31. The set cannot be described by n = 8 for instance since at least one of the BSSIDs in the range 0–255 might be
45 used as a BSSID by an AP that does not share the same operating class, channel, receive antenna connector, and transmit
46 antenna connector.
47

49 11.21 Tunneled direct-link setup
50
51
11.21.1 General
52
53
54 Change the 7th paragraph as follows:
55
56
Features, excluding PCO, that are not supported by the BSS but that are supported by both TDLS peer STAs
57
may be used on a TDLS direct link between those STAs regardless of whether or not they are supported by
58
59 the BSS. An example is the use of an HT-MCS on a TDLS direct link between HT STAs when these STAs
60 are associated with a non-HT BSS. Features that are supported by the BSS shall follow the BSS rules when
61 they are used on a TDLS direct link on the base channel. The channel width of a TDLS direct link on the
62
base channel shall not exceed the channel width of the BSS to which the TDLS peer STAs are associated,
63
except when the TDLS Wider Bandwidth subfield in the Extended Capabilities element of the TDLS Setup
64
65 Request frame or the TDLS Setup Response frame is 1 for both TDLS peer STAs.

11.22 Wireless network management procedures

11.22.2 Event request and report procedures

11.22.2.1 Event request and event report
15
16
17 Change the 1st paragraph as follows:
18
19
20 The Event Request and Event Report frames enable network real-time diagnostics. A STA whose
21
dot11EventsActivated equal to true shall support event requests and reports and shall set to 1 the Event field
22
of the Extended Capabilities elements that it transmits. If dot11EventsActivated is true and the Event Type
23
24 field is neither BSS Color Collision nor BSS Color In Use, a STA shall log all Transition, RSNA, peer-to-
25 peer, and WNM log events, including the corresponding TSF, UTC Offset and Event Time Error. An HE
26 STA that has dot11EventsActivated equal to true and reports BSS color collisions shall log all BSS color
27
collision events, including the TSF value when the STA finished logging the events that are reported (see
28
11.22.2.7 (BSS color collision event)).
29
30
31
A STA with either dot11AutonomousBSSColorCollisionReportingImplemented equal to true or dot11Au-
32
tonomousBSSColorInUseReportingImplemented equal to true shall set the Event field of the Extended
33
34 Capabilities elements that it transmits to 1.
35
36
37 Insert two new subclauses at the end of 11.22.2:
38
39
11.22.2.7 BSS color collision event
40
41
42
The BSS color collision event report enables a non-AP HE STA to inform its associated AP whether a BSS
43
color collision has occurred. The report carries information about the BSS color used by OBSSs that the
44
45 reporting STA is able to detect (see 26.17.3.5.2 (Autonomous reporting of BSS color collision)).
46
47
48 11.22.2.8 BSS color in use event
49
50
If a non-AP HE STA communicates with a peer STA with a BSS color that is different from the BSS color
51
52 used by its associated AP, the non-AP HE STA may send a BSS color in use event report to its associated
53 AP in which the Event Report field has the BSS color used in the communication with the peer STA.
54
55
56 A non-AP HE STA shall send a BSS color in use event report to its associated AP with the Event Report
57 field set to 0 to cancel a previously sent BSS color in use event report.
58
59
60 An AP shall not transmit frames to a non-AP HE STA during a TXOP, if it has received a BSS color in use
61 event report from that non-AP HE STA with a nonzero BSS color in the Event Report field and the AP
62
ignores an inter-BSS PPDU with the same BSS color value as the one carried in the Event Report field to
63
obtain a TXOP by following the procedure in 26.10.2.2 (General operation with non-SRG OBSS PD level)
64
65 and 26.10.2.3 (General operation with SRG OBSS PD level).

1 11.22.6 Fine timing measurement (FTM) procedure

11.22.6.4 Measurement exchange

7
8
For the Fine Timing Measurement frames transmitted during the FTM session:
9
10 — The responding STA shall not use a bandwidth wider than that indicated by the STA in the initial
11 Fine Timing Measurement frame.
12
13 — The responding STA shall not use an HE format if the STA indicated VHT or HT-mixed or non-HT
14 format in the initial Fine Timing Measurement frame.
15
— The responding STA shall not use a VHT format if the STA indicated HT-mixed or non-HT format

22 11.22.7 BSS transition management for network load balancing
23
24
11.22.7.1 BSS transition capability
25
26
27 Change the 3rd paragraph as follows:
28
29
Implementation of BSS transition management is optional for a WNM STA that is not a non-AP HE STA. A
30
STA that implements BSS transition management has dot11BSSTransitionImplemented equal to true. When
31
32 If dot11BSSTransitionImplemented is true, dot11WirelessManagementImplemented shall be true. A STA
33 with dot11BSSTransitionActivated equal to true shall support BSS transition management and shall set to 1
34 the BSS Transition field of the Extended Capabilities elements that it transmits. A non-AP HE STA shall
35
have dot11BSSTransitionImplemented and dot11BSSTransitionActivated equal to true.
36
37
38 11.22.7.4 BSS transition management response
39
40
Change the 2nd paragraph as follows:
41
42
43 The STA’s SME may include the result of its BSS transition decision in the Target BSSID field and BTM
44 Status Code field in the MLME-BTM.response primitive. A BTM Status Code field set to a value of 0 (i.e.,
45 Accept) indicates the STA will transition from the current BSS. If a non-HE STA’s SME receives an
46
MLME-BTM.indication primitive indicating a BSS transition management request that it is unable to com-
47
ply with, it may issue an MLME-BTM.response primitive with a status code indicating rejection may issue
48
49 an MLME-BTM.response primitive with a valid status code not equal to a value of 0 (i.e., Accept) indicating
50 rejection if it is unable to comply with this BSS transition management request.
51
52
Insert a new subclause at the end of 11.22.7 as follows:
53
54
55 11.22.7.5 Planned ESS
56
57
The AP may transmit an ESS Report element (see 9.4.2.256 (ESS Report element)) to indicate whether or
58
59 not it is in a planned ESS, to assist associated STAs' roaming. If it indicates that it is in a planned ESS, it
60 indicates whether or not it is at the physical edge of the ESS and can provide a recommendation on the RSSI
61 level to consider for BSS transition.
62
63
If the AP transmits an ESS Report element, it shall set the Planned ESS subfield in the ESS Information field
64
65 to 1 if it is part of an ESS that is planned with several BSSs in overlapping configuration, whereby an asso-
324 Copyright © 2020 IEEE. All rights reserved.
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.

## 第 325 页

IEEE P802.11ax/D8.0, October 2020
1 ciated STA may adjust its BSS transition algorithms accordingly. Otherwise, it shall set the Planned ESS
2 subfield to 0.
3
4
5 If the Planned ESS subfield is 1, then the AP shall set the Edge Of ESS subfield in the ESS Information field
6 of the ESS Report element to 1 if the AP’s BSS is at the edge of an ESS (e.g., exit of a building). Otherwise,
7 it shall set the Edge Of ESS subfield to 0.
8
9 NOTE—A non-AP STA could then prepare for more aggressive roaming or the device the non-AP STA is in could then
10 prepare for switching to a different system. However, how the non-AP STA uses the edge of ESS information is imple-
11 mentation specific and beyond the scope of this standard.
12
13
If the Planned ESS subfield is 1, then the AP shall set the Recommended BSS Transition RSSI Threshold
14
Within ESS subfield in the ESS Information field of the ESS Report element to indicate the beacon RSSI
15
16 below which an associated STA should initiate a BSS transition. Otherwise, it shall set the Recommended
17 BSS Transition RSSI Threshold Within ESS subfield to 0.
18
19 NOTE—A non-AP STA could then use the Recommended BSS Transition RSSI Threshold Within ESS subfield to
20 modify when it starts scanning for a new BSS. However, how the non-AP STA adjusts its BSS transition algorithms is
21 implementation specific and beyond the scope of this standard.
22
23
The value of the Edge Of ESS subfield and the Recommended BSS Transition RSSI Threshold Within ESS
24
subfield may be changed by the AP if conditions in the ESS change. An AP shall not change the value of the
25
26 Planned ESS subfield over the lifetime of the BSS.
27

29 11.32 Multi-band operation
30
31
11.32.5 On-channel Tunneling (OCT) operation
32
33
34 Change paragraphs 1-2 as follows:
35
36
37 OCT allows a STA of a multi-band capable device to transmit an MMPDU that was constructed by a differ-
38 ent STA of the same device. OCT provides the following:
39
— allows a STA of a multi-band capable device or a STA that is in the same device as another STA to
40
41 transmit or forward an MMPDU that was constructed by, addressed by or addressed to a different
42 STA in the same device
43
44 — allows an AP to transmit or forward an MMPDU that was constructed by, addressed by, or addressed
45 to another AP if either one of the APs sends a Reduced Neighbor Report element with a TBTT Infor-
46 mation field describing the other AP or if either one of the APs sends a Neighbor Report element
47
describing the other AP, and where both APs support OCT
48
49
50 An MMPDU transmitted this way is referred to as an OCT MMPDU. The MLME of the nontransmitting
51 STA that constructs or is the destination of an OCT MMPDU is referred to as an NT-MLME. The MLME of
52
the STA that transmits or receives an OCT MMPDU over the air is referred to as a TR-MLME. An NT-
53
MLME that constructs an OCT MMPDU destined to a peer NT-MLME does so according to the capabilities
54
55 of the STA that contains the peer NT-MLME.
56
NOTE—OCT can be used in conjunction with or independent from the FST setup protocol.
57
58
59 Either of the following conditions indicates that a STA supports OCT and has dot11OCTOptionImple-
60 mented equal to true:
61
62 — A STA supports OCT if the OCT Not Supported subfield within the STA’s Multi-band element is 0.
63
— A non-AP STA supports OCT if the OCT field is equal to true in the Extended Capabilities elements
64
65 it transmits.
Copyright © 2020 IEEE. All rights reserved. 325
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.

## 第 326 页

IEEE P802.11ax/D8.0, October 2020
1 — If a reporting AP sends a frame with a Reduced Neighbor Report element with a TBTT Information
2 field describing a reported AP that has the OCT Recommended subfield equal to 1, then both the
3
reporting AP and the reported AP support OCT.
4
5 — If a reporting AP sends a frame with a Neighbor Report element describing a reported AP that has
6 the OCT Supported With Reporting AP subfield equal to 1, then both the reporting AP and the
7
reported AP support OCT.
8
9
10 A STA should not perform OCT with a peer STA that does not support OCT. A STA that does not support
11 OCT shall ignore a received OCT MMPDU.
12
13
Change the 9th paragraph as follows:
14
15
16 To transmit a tunneled MMPDU, the SME of a multi-band capable device STA that supports OCT generates
17 an OCT MLME request primitive that includes the peer Multi-band element and the local Multi-band ele-
18
ment. If the OCT MLME request primitive is the MLME-SCAN.request primitive with ScanType parameter
19
set to ACTIVE, the BSSID field within the peer Multi-band element shall be set to the value of the BSSID
20
21 parameter in the MLME-SCAN.request primitive and the BSSID field within the local Multi-band element
22 shall be set to an individual MAC address.

29
30 11.46.2.1 FILS Discovery frame transmission
31
32
33 Change the 3rd and 4th paragraph as follows:
34
35 If an AP transmits a FILS Discovery frame as a non-HT duplicate PPDU in an 80+80 MHz channel band-
36
width, the Channel Center Frequency Segment 1 field shall be present in the FILS Discovery frame and shall
37
be set to the channel center frequency of the frequency segment 1 for an 80+80 MHz VHT or HE operating
38
39 channel.
40
41
A FILS AP should transmit FILS Discovery frame(s) in every beacon interval. The interval between the
42
transmission of a Beacon frame and a subsequent FILS Discovery frame shall be no less than the interval
43
44 indicated in dot11FILSFDFrameBeaconMinimumInterval. The transmission interval between subsequent
45 FILS Discovery frames by an AP in a beacon interval shall be no less than the interval indicated in dot11-
46 FILSFDFrameBeaconMinimumInterval. If dot11FILSFDFrameBeaconMaximumInteval is not equal to 0,
47
and if a Beacon frame, broadcast Probe Response frame or FD frame has not been transmitted by an AP for
48
a period that is equal to dot11FILSFDFrameBeaconMaximumInterval, that AP shall queue for transmission
49
50 a FD frame, broadcast Probe Response frame or a Beacon frame unless the next TBTT is within a duration
51 indicated by the value of dot11FILSFDFrameBeaconMinimumInterval.
52
53
Insert the following at the end of the subclause:
54
55
56 The Address 1 field of the FILS Discovery frame shall be set to the broadcast address.
57
58
For the APs in a multiple BSSID set, only the AP corresponding to the transmitted BSSID may transmit a
59
FILS Discovery frame; other APs corresponding to nontransmitted BSSIDs shall not transmit a FILS Dis-
60
61 covery frame. If dot11MultiBSSIDImplemented is true, then the following applies to the fields in the FILS
62 Discovery frame:
63
— The SSID or Short SSID field shall be set to the SSID or short SSID, respectively, of the transmitted
64
65 BSSID
326 Copyright © 2020 IEEE. All rights reserved.
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.

## 第 327 页

IEEE P802.11ax/D8.0, October 2020
1 — The FILS Capability field shall be present and the Multiple BSSIDs Presence Indicator subfield shall
2 be set to 1
3
4
A 6 GHz AP that is sending FILS Discovery frames shall include at least one Transmit Power Envelope ele-
5
6 ment, where the Maximum Transmit Power Category subfield is set to 0 (Default), including a regulatory
7 client transmit power limit for at least 20 MHz bandwidth (for EIRP) or at least the AP's primary 20 MHz
8 channel (for EIRP PSD).
9
10 NOTE 1—The AP is not required to include power constraints for bandwidths greater than 20 MHz in FILS Discovery
11 frames, even if they are supported by the BSS. Therefore, when a Transmit Power Envelope element in a FILS Discov-
12 ery frame indicates EIRP PSD limits, the value of N (see Table9-276a (Meaning of Maximum Transmit Power Count
13 subfield if the Maximum Transmit Power Interpretation subfield is 1 or 3)) can take any valid value equal to or less than
14 the BSS bandwidth in MHz divided by 20.
15
16 NOTE 2—A Transmit Power Envelope element sent in a FILS Discovery frame by a 6 GHz AP can be used by a STA to
17 determine a transmit power limit for 20 MHz PPDUs corresponding to the 6 GHz AP prior to having received a Beacon
18 or Probe Response frame from that AP. A STA might, for example, determine a transmit power limit based on this infor-
19 mation when sending a Probe Request frame with 20 MHz PPDU bandwidth during active scan on the 6 GHz AP's chan-
20 nel.

11.49 Reduced neighbor report
23
24
25 Change the 1st paragraph as follows:
26
27
In Beacon and Probe Response frames, a Reduced Neighbor Report element may be transmitted by an AP
28
29 with dot11TVHTOptionImplemented or dot11FILSActivated true. In FILS Discovery frames, a Reduced
30 Neighbor Report element is optionally sent by a FILS AP. A Reduced Neighbor Report element contains
31 information on neighbor APs, co-located APs or a combination of both. An AP that operates in the 2.4 GHz
32
or 5 GHz band and that is in the same co-located AP set as one or more 6 GHz APs shall follow the rules in
33
11.53 (Out of band discovery of a 6 GHz BSS) for including a Reduced Neighbor Report element in Beacon
34
35 and Probe Response frames. A Reduced Neighbor Report element might not be exhaustive either by choice
36 or by the fact that there may be neighbor APs not known to the reporting AP. An AP that intends to report
37 neighboring or co-located APs may include more than one Reduced Neighbor Report element in a Beacon,
38
Probe Response or FILS Discovery frame, if the reported APs do not all fit in a single Reduced Neighbor
39
Report element; otherwise, it shall not include more than one Reduced Neighbor Report element.
40
41
42 An AP with dot11MultiBSSIDImplemented equal to true shall not include Reduced Neighbor Report ele-
43 ment in the Nontransmitted BSSID Profile subelement of the Multiple BSSID element.
44
45 NOTE—The Beacon, Probe Response or FILS Discovery frame of an AP with dot11MultiBSSIDImplemented equal
46 true can carry a Reduced Neighbor Report element. In this case, the values of the fields in the Reduced Neighbor Report
47 element apply to all the BSSs in the multiple BSSID set except for the Same SSID subfield(s), whose value applies only
48 to the BSS corresponding to the transmitted BSSID.
49
50 Insert the following paragraphs after the 1st paragraph
51
52
53 If an AP reported in a TBTT Information field in a Reduced Neighbor Report element is not part of a multi-
54 ple BSSID set, then the BSS Parameters subfield, if included, shall have the Multiple BSSID subfield set to
55 0. If an AP reported in a TBTT Information field in a Reduced Neighbor Report element is a transmitted
56
BSSID, then the BSS Parameters subfield, if included, shall have the Multiple BSSID subfield set to 1 and
57
the Transmitted BSSID subfield set to 1. If an AP reported in a TBTT Information field in a Reduced Neigh-
58
59 bor Report element is a nontransmitted BSSID, then the BSS Parameters subfield, if included, shall have the
60 Multiple BSSID subfield set to 1 and the Transmitted BSSID subfield set to 0.
61
62
An HE AP with dot11MultiBSSIDImplemented equal to true may advertise one or more nontransmitted
63
BSSIDs in the multiple BSSID set by including the Reduced Neighbor Report element in its Beacon, Probe
64
65 Response or FILS Discovery frames with the BSS Parameters subfield of the TBTT Information field con-

29
30
31 A STA that receives a Neighbor AP Information field with a recognized TBTT Information Field Type sub-
32
field but an unrecognized TBTT Information Length subfield shall ignore that Neighbor AP Information
33

34

36 sequent Neighbor AP Information fields or (2) process the first 13 octets of each TBTT Information field of

38
remaining TBTT Information Length minus 13 octets of each TBTT Information field of the Neighbor AP
39
Information field, and continue to process the subsequent Neighbor AP Information fields. If the unrecog-
40
41 nized TBTT Information Length value is less than or equal to 13, the STA shall follow alternative (1). If the
42 unrecognized TBTT Information Length value is greater than 13, an HE STA shall follow alternative (2) and
43 a non-HE STA shall follow either alternative (1) or (2).

element(s) transmitted by the reported AP in Beacon and Probe Response frames.

62 by an AP that is in the same co-located AP set as a 6 GHz AP can be used by a STA to determine a local transmit power
63 limit for 20 MHz PPDUs corresponding to a 6 GHz AP, prior to having received a Beacon or Probe Response frame
64 from that AP. The value in the 20 MHz PSD subfield can be used by any STA, although for some categories it may result
65 in determination of a lower transmit power limit than would be determined by (subsequent) reception of a Transmit

11.53 Out of band discovery of a 6 GHz BSS
8
9
10 An AP that operates in the 2.4 GHz or 5 GHz band and that is in the same co-located AP set as one or more
11 6 GHz APs shall include in Beacon and Probe Response frames that it transmits a Reduced Neighbor Report
12 element with the Co-Located AP subfield in the BSS Parameters subfield in the TBTT Information field set
13
to 1 to provide at least the operating channels and operating classes of those 6 GHz APs.
14
15
NOTE—The Reduced Neighbor Report element might contain information on 6 GHz APs that are not in the same co-
16
located AP set as the transmitting AP. In this case the Co-Located AP subfield is set to 0.
17
18
An AP responds to a probe request by following the rules defined in 11.1.4.3.4 (Criteria for sending a
19
20 response).
21
22 If neither of the following conditions is met:
23
24 — the AP transmits an individually addressed Probe Response frame to a STA that has signaled that it
25 does not support operating in the 6 GHz band (see 9.4.2.53 (Supported Operating Classes element))
26
— the AP operating in the 6 GHz band does not intend to be discovered by STAs
27
28
29 then the following applies:
30
— If an AP operating in the 2.4 GHz or 5 GHz band is in the same co-located AP set as one or more
31
32 6GHz APs and has the same SSID as those 6 GHz APs, then the Beacon and Probe Response frames
33 transmitted by the AP or by the transmitted BSSID of the same Multiple BSSID set as the AP shall
34 include, for each of these 6 GHz APs, a TBTT Information field in a Reduced Neighbor Report ele-
35
ment with the BSSID field set to the BSSID of the 6 GHz AP, and with either the Short SSID field
36
set to the short SSID of the 6 GHz AP or the Same SSID subfield in the BSS Parameters subfield set
37
38 to 1
39
— If an AP operating in the 2.4 GHz or 5 GHz band is in the same co-located AP set as a 6 GHz AP and
40
has a different SSID, and no other AP in the same co-located AP set and operating in the 2.4GHz or
41
42 5 GHz band is indicating the 6 GHz AP in a Reduced Neighbor Report element of the Beacon and
43 Probe Response frames they transmit, then Beacon and Probe Response frames transmitted by the
44 AP (or by the transmitted BSSID of the same Multiple BSSID set as the AP) shall include a TBTT
45
Information field in a Reduced Neighbor Report element with the BSSID field and the Short SSID
46
field set to the BSSID and short SSID of the 6 GHz AP, respectively.
47
48
49 If the AP reported in the TBTT Information field in the Reduced Neighbor Report element is a 6 GHz AP,
50 the reporting AP shall include the BSS Parameters subfield in the TBTT Information field and shall follow
51
the rules in 11.49 (Reduced neighbor report) to set the Multiple BSSID subfield, the Transmitted BSSID
52
subfield, the Co-Located AP subfield and the OCT Recommended subfield.

56

59 AP.

1 12. Security
2
3
4 12.5 RSNA confidentiality and integrity protocols
5
6
7 12.5.3 CTR with CBC-MAC protocol (CCMP)
8
9
12.5.3.3 CCMP cryptographic encapsulation
10
11
12 12.5.3.3.2 PN processing
13
14
Change the 1st paragraph as follows:
15
16
17 The PN is incremented by a positive number for each MPDU. The PN shall be incremented in steps of 1 for
18 constituent MPDUs of fragmented MSDUs, A-MSDUs and MMPDUs. For PV0 MPDUs, the PN shall
19
never repeat for a series of encrypted MPDUs using the same temporal key. For PV1 MPDUs, the PN shall
20
never repeat for a series of encrypted MPDUs using the same temporal key and TID/ACI.
21
22
23 12.5.5 GCM protocol (GCMP)
24
25
12.5.5.3 GCMP cryptographic encapsulation
26
27
28 12.5.5.3.2 PN processing
29
30
Change the 1st paragraph as follows:
31
32
33 The PN is incremented by a positive number for each MPDU. The PN shall be incremented in steps of 1 for
34 constituent MPDUs of fragmented MSDUs, A-MSDUs and MMPDUs. The PN shall never repeat for a
35
series of encrypted MPDUs using the same temporal key.
36
37
38 Insert the following at the end if Clause 12:
39
40
41 12.12 Constraints on allowed security parameters
42
43
44 12.12.1 Introduction
45
46 This standard defines various security mechanisms of which some are deprecated or obsolete and are not
47
considered to meet current requirements for security. Such mechanisms enable interoperability with legacy
48
devices, but are not suitable for new uses where legacy support is not needed. This subclause describes con-
49
50 straints on security parameter selection for some use cases.
51
52
12.12.2 Security constraints in the 6 GHz band
53
54
55 The following apply to a STA operating in the 6 GHz band:
56
— The STA shall not use the following pre-RSNA security methods: WEP, Open System authentica-
57
tion without encryption, Shared Key authentication
58
59 — The STA shall not use the following cipher suite selectors:
60
61 • 00-0F-AC:0 (Use group cipher suite)
62 • 00-0F-AC:1 (WEP-40)
63
• 00-0F-AC:2 (TKIP)
64
65 • 00-0F-AC:5 (WEP-104)
Copyright © 2020 IEEE. All rights reserved. 331
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.

## 第 332 页

IEEE P802.11ax/D8.0, October 2020
1 — The STA should use Opportunistic Wireless Encryption, as specified in IETF RFC 8110, when con-
2 necting in an infrastructure BSS without authentication (as a replacement for Open System authenti-
3
cation without encryption)
4
5 — The STA shall not use the following AKM suite selectors:
6
• 00-0F-AC:2 (PSK)
7
• 00-0F-AC:4 (FT authentication using PSK)
8
9 • 00-0F-AC:6 (PSK with SHA-256)
10 • 00-0F-AC:19 (FT authentication using PSK with SHA-384)
11 • 00-0F-AC:20 (PSK with SHA-384)
12
— The STA should use SAE (AKM suite selectors 00-0F-AC:8 and/or 00-0F-AC:9) if authenticating
13
14 using a password where IEEE Std 802.1X is not used (as a replacement for PSK)
15
— The STA shall use management frame protection (MFPR=1) when using RSN
16

14.2.4 Mesh STA configuration
7
8
9 Change as follows:
10
11
The mesh STA configuration consists of the mesh profile (see 14.2.3 (Mesh profile)), the Supported Rates
12
and BSS Membership Selectors element, the Extended Supported Rates and BSS Membership Selectors ele-
13
14 ment, the HT Operations element (if present), and the VHT Operations element (if present), and the HE
15 Operation element (if present).
16
17
Mesh STA configurations are identical if the following conditions hold:
18
19 — The mesh profiles are identical.
20
— The BSSBasicRateSet parameter of the MLME-START.request primitive is identical to the basic
21
rate set indicated by the Supported Rates and BSS Membership Selectors element and Extended Sup-
22
23 ported Rates and BSS Membership Selectors element, if present, received in the MLME-MESH-
24 PEERINGMANAGEMENT.indication primitive.

26
27 START.request primitive is identical to the HT Operation element received in the MLME-MESH-
28 PEERINGMANAGEMENT.indication primitive.

30
MLME-START.request primitive are identical to the Basic VHT-MCS and NSS fields in the VHT
31
32 Operation element received in the MLME-MESHPEERINGMANAGEMENT.indication primitive.

— For HE mesh STAs, the Basic HE-MCS and NSS Set field in the HE Operation element of the
34
MLME-START.request primitive are identical to the Basic HE-MCS and NSS Set field in the HE
35
36 Operation element received in the MLME-MESHPEERINGMANAGEMENT.indication primitive.
